title: Epic: Phase 5 — Stack-Agnostic Templates (remove Python assumptions from beads templates and generated output)
description:
> **Before Starting:** Always groom the epic first. Ensure the goal is clear,
> success metrics are measurable, scope is well-defined, and child tasks are planned.

## Goal / Problem

The beads ticket template (`docs/beads_templates/beads-ticket-template.md`) and epic template hardcode Python-specific content that leaks into every generated ticket, regardless of the target project's stack:

- TDD examples use `python` code blocks, `tests/domain/test_feature.py`, `uv run pytest`
- GREEN phase references `src/domain/models/feature.py`
- Quality Gates section hardcodes `uv run ruff check`, `uv run mypy`, `uv run pytest`
- Edge case examples use Python syntax (`""`, `None`)
- Epic template AC section hardcodes the same Python quality gate commands

alty's templates serve two purposes:
1. **alty's own workflow** (Python) — these hardcoded values are correct for us
2. **Generated tickets for user projects** (any stack) — these values are wrong

The template structure (DDD Alignment, SOLID Mapping, TDD Workflow, Quality Gates) is universal. The concrete examples and commands are stack-specific.

## DDD Alignment

| Bounded Context | Impact |
|----------------|--------|
| Ticket Pipeline | TicketDetailRenderer uses StackProfile for quality gates but TDD examples are hardcoded |
| Tool Translation | Generated CLAUDE.md quality gates already use StackProfile (verify) |
| Bootstrap | Beads templates are copied into user projects during init |

## Success Metrics

- Generated tickets for non-Python projects contain no Python-specific commands
- Template structure (sections, workflow) remains identical across stacks
- alty's own templates still work for Python (no regression)
- Quality gates section in generated tickets uses StackProfile commands
- Epic and ticket templates are stack-agnostic

## Scope

**In scope**
- Make beads ticket template stack-aware (parameterize Python-specific content)
- Make beads epic template stack-aware
- Update TicketDetailRenderer to use StackProfile for TDD workflow section
- Verify generated CLAUDE.md quality gates come from StackProfile

**Out of scope**
- Changing alty's own internal workflow (we use Python, that's fine)
- New stack profiles (Go, Rust, TypeScript)
- Spike template (already stack-agnostic — research questions are universal)

## Phases / Milestones

1. Audit — identify all Python-specific content in templates and renderers
2. Refactor — parameterize templates, update renderers
3. Validate — verify generated output for GenericProfile vs PythonUvProfile

## Child Tasks (planned)

- jb0.1: Make TDD Workflow section in TicketDetailRenderer stack-aware
- jb0.2: Make Quality Gates section in beads templates stack-aware
- jb0.3: Validate generated output end-to-end for GenericProfile

## Dependencies

- alty-5li (Phase 3 — Stack-Aware Pipeline) ✓ closed

## Risks / Unknowns

- How much of the TDD section is truly universal vs Python-specific? (TDD principles are universal, but RED/GREEN/REFACTOR examples use Python syntax)
- Should alty generate stack-specific TDD examples, or just state principles and let the project fill in?

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All child tasks closed (`bd close <id>`) — each child must have passed quality gates and QA before close
- [ ] Generated tickets for GenericProfile contain no `uv run`, `pytest`, `pyproject.toml` references
- [ ] Generated tickets for PythonUvProfile still contain correct Python commands
- [ ] Template structure (Goal, DDD, SOLID, TDD, Steps, AC, Edge Cases, QG) unchanged
- [ ] Documentation updated where required
- [ ] For all code changes (in child tasks), quality gates were run before each task was closed
- [ ] QA was done for each child task before close

title: Make TDD Workflow section in TicketDetailRenderer stack-aware via StackProfile
description:
> **Before Starting:** Always groom the ticket first. Ensure the goal is clear,
> acceptance criteria are testable, and steps are well-defined before assigning work.

> **Freshness:** If this ticket has a `review_needed` label, read the ripple comments
> (`bd comments <id>`) before starting work. Present review results to the user and
> clear the flag before claiming the ticket.

## Goal / Problem

The TDD Workflow section in TicketDetailRenderer._render_tdd_section() is hardcoded with generic text that doesn't reference any stack-specific commands. However, the _render_full() and _render_standard() methods embed quality gate commands from StackProfile — the TDD section should also optionally include stack-appropriate test runner examples.

Currently _render_tdd_section() outputs:
- RED: "Write failing tests for each invariant"
- GREEN: "Implement minimal code to pass"
- REFACTOR: "Clean up while tests stay green"

This is already mostly stack-agnostic. The issue is that the beads ticket TEMPLATE (not the renderer) has hardcoded Python examples that get used when creating tickets manually. The renderer and template need to be consistent.

## Background / Context

- TicketDetailRenderer at src/domain/services/ticket_detail_renderer.py
- Beads ticket template at docs/beads_templates/beads-ticket-template.md
- StackProfile at src/domain/models/stack_profile.py provides quality_gate_commands

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Pipeline |
| Ubiquitous Language | TicketDetailRenderer, StackProfile, TicketDetailLevel |
| Layer | Domain (ticket_detail_renderer.py) |

## Design

### Approach

The TDD Workflow section in the renderer is already generic. The fix is primarily in the template files:

1. Replace Python-specific examples in beads-ticket-template.md with placeholders
2. Add a StackProfile property for test runner command (for renderer use)
3. Keep TDD principles universal, make examples stack-aware

### Data Models

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| StackProfile | Protocol | Add `test_command_example` property |

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | TicketDetailRenderer renders tickets, StackProfile provides stack details |
| **O**pen/Closed | New stack profiles add their own examples without modifying renderer |
| **D**ependency Inversion | Renderer depends on StackProfile protocol, not concrete profiles |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase
- Test that _render_tdd_section() with GenericProfile produces no Python-specific content
- Test that _render_tdd_section() with PythonUvProfile produces Python examples

### GREEN Phase
- Update _render_tdd_section() to accept optional StackProfile
- Add test_command_example to StackProfile

### REFACTOR Phase
- Ensure all quality gates pass

## Steps

1. Add test_command_example property to StackProfile protocol, PythonUvProfile, GenericProfile
2. Update _render_tdd_section() to accept profile and use test_command_example
3. Update beads-ticket-template.md TDD section to use generic placeholder
4. Update tests

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] _render_tdd_section() produces stack-appropriate examples when profile provided
- [ ] GenericProfile TDD section has no Python-specific content
- [ ] PythonUvProfile TDD section includes Python test runner example
- [ ] beads-ticket-template.md TDD section uses generic placeholder
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Quality gates pass

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| No profile | None | Generic TDD principles only |
| GenericProfile | empty commands | TDD principles, no test runner example |
| PythonUvProfile | full commands | TDD principles + Python test runner |

## Quality Gates

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass with >= 80% coverage
- [ ] Edge cases have test coverage

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on alty-5li (Phase 3) ✓ closed

> **IMPORTANT:** Dependencies listed here are documentation only. You MUST also set
> formal dependencies with `bd dep add <this-ticket> <depends-on>` so that
> `bd blocked` / `bd ready` / ripple review can see them. Text-only deps are invisible
> to the dependency graph.

title: Make beads templates stack-agnostic — parameterize Python-specific examples and commands
description:
> **Before Starting:** Always groom the ticket first. Ensure the goal is clear,
> acceptance criteria are testable, and steps are well-defined before assigning work.

> **Freshness:** If this ticket has a `review_needed` label, read the ripple comments
> (`bd comments <id>`) before starting work. Present review results to the user and
> clear the flag before claiming the ticket.

## Goal / Problem

The beads templates (ticket, epic) contain Python-specific content. These templates are used in two contexts:

1. **alty's own workflow** — Python is correct
2. **Generated for user projects** — must be stack-agnostic

Python-specific content in beads-ticket-template.md:
- Line 72-79: python code block with tests/domain/test_feature.py
- Line 81: uv run pytest tests/domain/test_feature.py -v
- Line 85: src/domain/models/feature.py
- Line 89: uv run pytest tests/domain/test_feature.py -v
- Lines 123-125: uv run ruff, mypy, pytest hardcoded

Python-specific content in beads-epic-template.md:
- Lines 78-80: uv run ruff, mypy, pytest hardcoded in AC section

## Background / Context

- beads-ticket-template.md at docs/beads_templates/beads-ticket-template.md
- beads-epic-template.md at docs/beads_templates/beads-epic-template.md
- These templates define the structure that all tickets follow
- The DDD process sections (Goal, DDD Alignment, SOLID, TDD, Steps, AC) are universal
- Only the example code snippets and quality gate commands are stack-specific

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Pipeline, Bootstrap |
| Ubiquitous Language | TicketPlan, StackProfile, beads templates |
| Layer | docs/beads_templates/ (template files) |

## Design

### Approach

Replace Python-specific examples with generic placeholders. The templates are documentation (not code), so the fix is content replacement:

**TDD Workflow section** — replace Python examples with generic:
```
Write failing tests first using your project's test framework.
Run your test runner → should FAIL
```

**Quality Gates section** — replace hardcoded commands with:
```
Run your project's quality gates (lint, type check, tests).
See StackProfile for specific commands.
```

**Epic AC section** — same treatment for quality gate commands.

### Two-file approach

Keep templates as-is for alty's own use. When alty generates templates for user projects, it parameterizes the stack-specific sections via StackProfile.

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | Templates define structure, StackProfile provides commands |
| **O**pen/Closed | New stacks extend via new StackProfile, templates unchanged |
| **D**ependency Inversion | Templates reference abstract "your quality gates", not concrete commands |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase
- Verify current templates contain Python-specific content (baseline)
- Write test that generated ticket for GenericProfile has no Python commands

### GREEN Phase
- Update template content to use generic placeholders
- Update any code that copies templates into user projects

### REFACTOR Phase
- Ensure alty's own workflow (CLAUDE.md references) still works

## Steps

1. Update beads-ticket-template.md — replace Python examples with generic placeholders
2. Update beads-epic-template.md — replace Python quality gate commands with generic
3. Verify alty's own CLAUDE.md still references correct Python commands for our workflow
4. Update tests if any assert on template content

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] beads-ticket-template.md contains no Python-specific commands or file paths
- [ ] beads-epic-template.md contains no Python-specific commands
- [ ] TDD Workflow section uses generic test framework language
- [ ] Quality Gates section references project's quality gates generically
- [ ] alty's own CLAUDE.md still has correct Python commands for our workflow
- [ ] Template structure (sections, ordering) unchanged
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Quality gates pass

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| alty's own tickets | Using template directly | Still works (CLAUDE.md has Python commands) |
| User project tickets | Generated via pipeline | No Python-specific content |

## Quality Gates

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Edge cases have test coverage

## Risks / Dependencies

- Must not break alty's own grooming workflow
- CLAUDE.md for alty's own project has Python quality gates — that's separate from the template

> **IMPORTANT:** Dependencies listed here are documentation only. You MUST also set
> formal dependencies with `bd dep add <this-ticket> <depends-on>` so that
> `bd blocked` / `bd ready` / ripple review can see them. Text-only deps are invisible
> to the dependency graph.

title: Validate generated output end-to-end for GenericProfile vs PythonUvProfile
description:
> **Before Starting:** Always groom the ticket first. Ensure the goal is clear,
> acceptance criteria are testable, and steps are well-defined before assigning work.

> **Freshness:** If this ticket has a `review_needed` label, read the ripple comments
> (`bd comments <id>`) before starting work. Present review results to the user and
> clear the flag before claiming the ticket.

## Goal / Problem

After jb0.1 and jb0.2 are complete, validate that the full generated output (tickets, configs, gap reports) is truly stack-agnostic for GenericProfile and correctly stack-specific for PythonUvProfile.

This is a validation/QA ticket — no new features, just verification.

## Background / Context

- jb0.1 makes TicketDetailRenderer TDD section stack-aware
- jb0.2 makes beads templates stack-agnostic
- This ticket verifies the combined effect end-to-end

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Pipeline, Tool Translation, Rescue |
| Ubiquitous Language | StackProfile, GenericProfile, PythonUvProfile |
| Layer | Cross-cutting (all output paths) |

## Steps

1. Generate tickets for a fake DomainModel with GenericProfile — grep output for Python-specific terms
2. Generate tickets for same model with PythonUvProfile — verify Python commands present
3. Generate CLAUDE.md with GenericProfile — verify no Python commands
4. Generate CLAUDE.md with PythonUvProfile — verify Python commands present
5. Run alty init --existing on fake-astro — verify gap report has no Python assumptions
6. Document findings

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] GenericProfile generated tickets contain zero instances of: `uv run`, `pytest`, `ruff`, `mypy`, `pyproject.toml`, `tests/domain/`, `src/domain/models/`
- [ ] PythonUvProfile generated tickets contain correct Python commands
- [ ] GenericProfile CLAUDE.md has no quality gate commands
- [ ] PythonUvProfile CLAUDE.md has quality gate commands
- [ ] Gap report for non-Python project has no structure gaps
- [ ] All findings documented
- [ ] Quality gates pass

## Quality Gates

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on jb0.1 and jb0.2 completing first

> **IMPORTANT:** Dependencies listed here are documentation only. You MUST also set
> formal dependencies with `bd dep add <this-ticket> <depends-on>` so that
> `bd blocked` / `bd ready` / ripple review can see them. Text-only deps are invisible
> to the dependency graph.

title: Epic: alty Phase 1 — Foundation
description:
Bootstrap alty using its own process. Create PRD, DDD artifacts, Architecture, and identify spikes. No implementation until domain is understood.

## Success Metrics
- PRD complete and reviewed
- DDD.md with domain stories, ubiquitous language, bounded contexts
- Architecture.md informed by DDD artifacts
- All spikes identified and created as child tickets
- Knowledge base structure decided via spike

## Scope
- Phase 1 only: foundation docs + spike research
- No code implementation yet
- No MCP server yet (spike first)

title: Spike: Knowledge base structure for tool conventions
description:
## Research Question

How should we organize the knowledge base for AI coding tool conventions (Claude Code, Cursor, Antigravity, OpenCode) so docs are RLM-addressable, version-tracked (current + 3 previous major versions), and maintainable — including a foundation for drift detection?

## Timebox

**Timebox:** 4 hours

## Background / Context

- White-hacker agent uses RLM pattern: docs are addressable variables, not search targets
- Need to cover: config file formats, agent profile formats, command/rule formats, .gitignore patterns
- Must support 4 tools with version tracking
- Knowledge must be useful for both guided bootstrap AND ongoing development
- **Must include global config paths** — each tool has global settings (e.g. ~/.claude/) that override local project settings. Knowledge base must document these paths per tool per version so alty init can detect conflicts.
- **Tool translation is P0** (moved from P1 per k7m.9 research and PRD update). This spike directly feeds the P0 multi-tool config generation feature. The KB structure must support generation of domain-aware configs, not just reference docs.
- **Drift detection is P1** — The KB must be structured so that a future drift detection system can compare stored conventions against actual tool versions. Design the schema with this in mind even though detection logic is P1.
- **k7m.4 spike outputs** — CLI design defined KnowledgeLookupPort (application port), MCP resources alty://knowledge/*, and .alty/knowledge/ directory. This spike must align with those interfaces.
- Links: docs/DDD.md §Knowledge Base context, docs/research/20260222_cli_mcp_design.md §Knowledge Base

## Investigation Approach

1. Document each tool's config structure (files, formats, capabilities)
2. **Document global vs local config precedence per tool**
3. **Map global config paths** (e.g. ~/.claude/, ~/.cursor/) per OS
4. Identify commonalities and differences across tools
5. Design directory structure that supports RLM lookup
6. Design versioning scheme (how to track current + 3 prev)
7. **Design drift-detection-ready schema** — what metadata per convention entry enables future staleness checks (version ranges, changelog URLs, deprecation flags)?

## Expected Deliverables

- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_knowledge_base_structure.md
- [ ] Proposed directory structure for .alty/knowledge/
- [ ] RLM lookup table design
- [ ] **Global config path registry per tool** (path, what it controls, override behavior)
- [ ] Version management strategy
- [ ] **Drift detection schema design** (metadata fields that enable future version comparison)
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Research question answered with clear recommendation for KB structure
- [ ] Findings documented in research report
- [ ] Directory structure aligns with KnowledgeLookupPort from k7m.4
- [ ] Schema supports RLM addressing (O(1) lookup by tool+version+topic)
- [ ] Drift detection metadata fields defined (even though detection logic is P1)
- [ ] Follow-up implementation tickets created (if applicable)
- [ ] Timebox respected (4 hours, or extended with justification)

title: Spike: Architecture fitness function generation design
description:
## Research Question

How should alty map a bounded context map to executable architecture fitness function tests (import-linter TOML contracts + pytestarch test files)?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background
Competitive research (k7m.9) identified Architecture Fitness Functions as a P0 launch feature -- the core technical moat. Research into Python architecture testing tools found:

- **import-linter** (v2.10, BSD-2) -- config-file based (TOML contracts in pyproject.toml). Contract types: layers, forbidden, independence, acyclic_siblings, protected, custom. Underlying engine: grimp (programmatic import graph API).
- **pytestarch** (v4.0.1, Apache-2.0) -- pure Python fluent API, integrates with pytest. Best candidate for programmatic test file generation.
- **deply** (v0.8.0, BSD-3) -- YAML-driven, also enforces naming conventions and generates Mermaid diagrams.

**Key finding:** Java has Context Mapper + ArchUnit + jMolecules 2.0 + Spring Modulith for DDD-to-architecture-test pipelines. Python has NO equivalent. alty would be building novel capability.

**Risk:** import-linter Python API is intentionally minimal (read-only config). Generating contracts means generating TOML programmatically -- needs a Pydantic model layer to avoid string-manipulation bugs.

## Research Input: k7m.2 Treatment Levels per Classification
The k7m.2 consolidated research (docs/research/20260222_guided_ddd_question_framework_consolidated.md, Section 3) defines treatment levels that drive contract strictness:
- **Core**: layers + forbidden + independence + acyclic_siblings contracts (strict)
- **Supporting**: layers + basic forbidden contracts (moderate)
- **Generic**: single forbidden contract — ACL boundary only (minimal)

The generation pipeline must read the complexity budget YAML output and select contract types accordingly.

## Research Input: k7m.5 DDD Artifacts
The DDD (docs/DDD.md) defines the concrete input for this spike:
- **Bounded context names**: Guided Discovery, Domain Model, Architecture Testing, Ticket Pipeline, Ticket Freshness, Tool Translation, Knowledge Base, Bootstrap, Rescue
- **FitnessTestSuite aggregate** (Architecture Testing context) with 5 invariants:
  1. Every bounded context must have at least one contract
  2. Core subdomains must have all 4 contract types (layers, forbidden, independence, acyclic_siblings)
  3. Supporting must have at least layers + forbidden
  4. Generic must have at least one forbidden (ACL boundary)
  5. No contract may reference a module outside its bounded context except through defined relationships
- **Context map** defines the integration patterns (domain events, queries, ACLs) that contracts must enforce
- Use these as the concrete bounded context map schema rather than designing one from scratch

## Investigation Steps
1. Design the bounded context map schema (YAML/TOML) -- minimal input that captures enough to emit contracts
2. Map DDD concepts to import-linter contract types:
   - Bounded context boundaries -> layer contracts
   - Dependency direction rules -> forbidden import contracts
   - Aggregate isolation -> independence contracts
3. Map DDD concepts to pytestarch rules:
   - Domain layer purity -> no-import rules
   - Port/adapter separation -> naming + import rules
4. Design the generation pipeline: bounded context map -> intermediate model -> test files
5. Evaluate: import-linter TOML vs pytestarch .py files vs both
6. Prototype: generate sample contracts from a sample bounded context map

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_fitness_function_design.md
- [ ] Bounded context map input schema (minimal effective format)
- [ ] DDD concept -> architecture test mapping table
- [ ] Generation pipeline design (input -> intermediate model -> output files)
- [ ] ADR: import-linter vs pytestarch vs hybrid approach
- [ ] Sample: bounded context map + generated test files
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation

title: Spike: Domain story to ticket pipeline design
description:
## Research Question

How should alty auto-generate dependency-ordered beads tickets from DDD artifacts (domain stories, bounded contexts, aggregates)?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background
Competitive research (k7m.9) identified Domain Story to Ticket Pipeline as a P0 launch feature. This is what makes alty more than a doc generator -- it produces actionable, ordered work items.

The pipeline should:
- Read DDD artifacts (bounded contexts, aggregates, domain events)
- Generate beads epics per bounded context
- Generate tasks ordered by aggregate dependency (foundation first)
- Pre-fill each ticket with TDD red/green/refactor phases
- Map SOLID principles per ticket
- Derive acceptance criteria from domain invariants
- Set blocking relationships from the context map

## Research Input: k7m.2 Complexity Budget Output
The k7m.2 consolidated research (docs/research/20260222_guided_ddd_question_framework_consolidated.md, Section 3) defines complexity budget YAML output that drives ticket detail levels:
- **Core**: Full AC, TDD phases, SOLID mapping, edge cases, >= 90% domain coverage
- **Supporting**: Standard AC, basic tests, >= 80% coverage
- **Generic**: Stub tickets ("integrate X"), verify boundary, >= 60% coverage

The ticket pipeline must read the complexity budget classification and generate appropriately detailed tickets per subdomain.

## Research Input: PRD Traceability via RLM Pattern (discovered during dogfooding)
During development, we found that ripple review catches *freshness* ("did a dependency change?") but NOT *completeness* ("does this ticket cover all PRD capabilities it should?"). A ticket (k7m.4) was missing P0 PRD items (doc-health commands) that were never added — no ripple would have caught this because it was missing from the start.

**Solution implemented for alty's own workflow:**  — an RLM-addressable command that maps every PRD P0/P1 capability to its bounded context and expected ticket scope. The grooming checklist step 2 now runs  to detect gaps.

**The ticket pipeline must generate this for users too:**
- **Generate a  command** as part of the seeded project, with:
  - A capability map derived from the generated PRD (each P0/P1 item gets an ID)
  - Bounded context mapping from the generated DDD artifacts
  - Expected ticket scope from the generated ticket plan
- **After generating all tickets, run the traceability check** — report any PRD capabilities not covered by any ticket (the "coverage gap" report)
- **Include PRD traceability as a grooming checklist step** in generated CLAUDE.md files
- **Two complementary safety nets for ticket health:**
  -  (RLM pattern) — catches *completeness gaps* ("was a PRD capability never assigned?")
  - Usage: bd-ripple <closed-ticket-id> [context summary]

Flags open siblings and dependents with review_needed after a ticket closes. (event-driven) — catches *freshness decay* ("did a dependency change the context?")
  - Both follow the same principle: documents/tickets as addressable variables, not search targets

## Research Input: k7m.5 DDD Artifacts
The DDD (docs/DDD.md) defines the concrete input for this spike:
- **TicketPlan aggregate** (Ticket Pipeline context) with 5 invariants:
  1. Every bounded context must produce exactly one epic
  2. Foundation aggregates (no dependencies) must be ordered first
  3. No circular dependencies in the generated ticket graph
  4. Core subdomain tickets must have full AC, TDD phases, and SOLID mapping
  5. No ticket may reference an aggregate from a different bounded context without a defined relationship
- **TicketDetailLevel value object**: full (Core), standard (Supporting), stub (Generic)
- **DomainModel aggregate** structure defines the input format — use docs/DDD.md sections as the machine-readable schema rather than designing a new one
- **DependencyOrder**: context map relationships define the build sequence

## Investigation Steps
1. Define the DDD artifact input format that feeds the pipeline
   - What minimum structure from docs/DDD.md is machine-readable?
   - YAML front matter? Structured markdown sections? Separate YAML file?
2. Design the ticket generation algorithm
   - How to determine dependency order from aggregate relationships?
   - How to split work into right-sized tickets (not too coarse, not too fine)?
   - How to detect which aggregates are foundational (no dependencies)?
3. Design ticket content templates
   - TDD phase mapping: which domain concept maps to which test type?
   - SOLID mapping: which principle applies to which ticket?
   - Acceptance criteria: how to derive from domain invariants?
4. Design the beads integration
   - Use bd create programmatically or generate JSONL directly?
   - How to set parent/child and dependency relationships?
5. Evaluate: fully automated vs human-in-the-loop (preview + confirm)

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ticket_pipeline_design.md
- [ ] DDD artifact input format specification
- [ ] Ticket generation algorithm design (ordering, sizing, dependencies)
- [ ] Ticket content template (TDD phases, SOLID mapping, acceptance criteria)
- [ ] Beads integration approach (programmatic vs JSONL)
- [ ] Sample: DDD artifacts -> generated ticket set
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation
notes:
**DOGFOODING INSIGHT (2026-02-23):** We discovered that all 27 dependencies in the k7m epic were described in ticket text but never formally set with `bd dep add`. This made `bd blocked` return empty and `bd ready` return everything — agents could pick up tickets with unmet prerequisites. Ripple review would also fail since it traverses the formal dependency graph.

**REQUIREMENT for ticket pipeline design:** The generated ticket pipeline MUST:
1. Call `bd dep add <ticket> <depends-on>` for every dependency, not just describe them in text
2. The BeadsWriterPort interface must include a `set_dependency(ticket_id, depends_on_id)` method
3. After writing all tickets, verify with `bd blocked` that the graph is correct
4. The DependencyOrder topological sort must produce both the ordering AND the formal dep-add commands

This is the difference between "documentation" and "enforcement" — text deps are documentation, formal deps are enforcement.

title: Spike: Ticket freshness and ripple review design
description:
## Research Question

How should alty implement event-driven ticket freshness detection (ripple review) so that completing a ticket automatically flags open dependents/siblings for re-review with a context diff?

## Timebox
4 hours

## Background

We experienced this problem first-hand: closing k7m.9 (killer features spike) changed the context for k7m.2, k7m.3, k7m.4, and others. We had to manually groom each ticket to align with the research findings. No tool in the market (Jira, Linear, Kiro, GitHub stale-bot) does event-based staleness detection -- all use time-based inactivity only.

Research (docs/research/20260222_backlog_freshness_ticket_decay.md) identified four principles:
1. **Deferred detail** -- far-term tickets are stubs, detailed AC added just before work starts
2. **Event-triggered ripple flag** -- on ticket close, flag open dependents with review_needed
3. **Lightweight freshness metadata** -- review_needed + last_reviewed fields per ticket
4. **Context diff** -- record what the closed ticket produced so reviewers know what changed

Design decision (approved): **Approach 2 -- Ripple Flag + Context Diff** with human-in-the-loop. Agents see flags and present suggested updates to the user for approval. No automatic ticket rewrites.

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Freshness |
| Aggregates | RippleReview (4 invariants — see docs/DDD.md § 6) |
| Ubiquitous Language | ripple review, freshness, context rot, review_needed, last_reviewed, context diff, two-tier generation, after-close protocol |
| Layer | Application (orchestration of close -> flag -> review flow) + Infrastructure (beads integration) |

## Investigation Steps

1. **Design the ripple review data model**
   - What fields are added to beads ticket schema? (review_needed, last_reviewed, ripple_context)
   - How is ripple_context structured? (summary text? list of deliverables? link to research report?)
   - Should review_needed carry the ID of the ticket that triggered it?

2. **Design the close-time hook**
   - On bd close, how to traverse the dependency graph (depends_on, siblings in same epic)
   - Should it also flag tickets with related_to relationships?
   - How to capture the context summary -- manual input at close time? Auto-extract from ticket description/notes?
   - Output format: terminal message listing flagged tickets

3. **Design the pick-up-time flow**
   - When an agent claims a flagged ticket (bd update --status in_progress), what happens?
   - Agent reads ripple_context, reads the completed ticket's output, drafts suggested updates
   - Presents suggestions to user: update description? update AC? change priority? no change needed?
   - User approves/modifies/dismisses
   - Flag cleared, last_reviewed updated

4. **Design alty ticket-health command**
   - Read-only report: count of review_needed tickets, oldest last_reviewed dates
   - Per-epic breakdown
   - Integration with alty doc-health (unified health dashboard?)

5. **Design two-tier ticket generation**
   - How does the ticket pipeline (k7m.11) generate stubs vs full tickets?
   - What qualifies as "near-term" vs "far-term"? (distance from ready state? priority? epic phase?)
   - Stub format: title + one-sentence summary + dependencies + epic link, no detailed AC

6. **Design the after-close protocol**
   - The full after-close sequence: (1) ripple review flagged tickets → (2) fix flagged tickets → (3) groom next ready ticket (includes PRD traceability) → (4) report to user
   - Two complementary safety nets: ripple review catches *freshness decay*, PRD traceability catches *completeness gaps*
   - Agents must not start work on a review_needed ticket without presenting the review first
   - The generated CLAUDE.md must include this protocol so agents follow it automatically
   - Design the protocol as a CLAUDE.md section that alty generates into every bootstrapped project

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Research report exists at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_ripple_review_design.md
- [ ] Ripple review data model defined (fields, schema, beads integration)
- [ ] Close-time hook designed with dependency traversal + context capture
- [ ] Pick-up-time flow designed with agent behavior + user approval UX
- [ ] alty ticket-health command designed (report format, per-epic breakdown)
- [ ] Two-tier generation rules defined (stub vs full ticket criteria)
- [ ] Updated beads ticket template includes freshness metadata fields
- [ ] **After-close protocol explicitly designed** — the 4-step sequence (ripple → fix flagged → groom next → report) is documented as a concrete specification that alty generates into CLAUDE.md of every bootstrapped project
- [ ] **After-close protocol includes PRD traceability** — grooming step must run `/prd-traceability` (or equivalent) to catch completeness gaps, not just freshness
- [ ] **After-close protocol is agent-enforceable** — design specifies that agents MUST run the protocol automatically after closing any ticket, without waiting for user to ask
- [ ] Follow-up implementation tickets created

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on beads supporting additional fields (review_needed, last_reviewed, ripple_context) -- may need beads schema extension or use of existing notes/metadata fields
- Depends on k7m.11 (ticket pipeline design) for two-tier generation integration
- Depends on prd-traceability command (.claude/commands/prd-traceability.md) for completeness checks
- Must not add bureaucratic overhead -- the process should cost < 2 minutes per ticket pick-up
notes: **PRD Traceability: C25** (Template-enforced ticket creation) — This spike's after-close protocol design must include template enforcement for follow-up tickets. See prd-traceability.md C25.

title: Set up Typer CLI entry point with subcommand stubs
description:
## Goal / Problem

Create the Typer CLI entry point for `alty` with all 15 subcommand stubs so that downstream tickets (k7m.15, k7m.16, k7m.17, k7m.22-k7m.26) can implement their command logic against a working CLI skeleton. Without this, no CLI command can be wired or tested end-to-end.

## Background / Context

- Spike k7m.4 selected Typer 0.24.1 as the CLI framework (ADR in `docs/research/20260222_cli_mcp_design.md`)
- CLI command tree defined in ARCHITECTURE.md §6.1 with 15 commands mapped to bounded contexts
- Entry point: `alty = "src.infrastructure.cli.main:app"` (pyproject.toml `[project.scripts]`)
- Two subcommand groups: `generate` (4 subcommands) and `persona` (2 subcommands)
- Each stub command prints a "not yet implemented" message and exits cleanly
- **Pattern reference:** ARCHITECTURE.md §6.1-6.3 for definitive CLI command tree and entry points

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | CLI Framework (Generic subdomain) |
| Ubiquitous Language | N/A — infrastructure wiring only; no domain terms exposed |
| Layer | Infrastructure (`src/infrastructure/cli/`) |

Generic subdomain: CLI argument parsing is commodity (Typer handles it). Stub ticket detail follows the Generic treatment level — minimal domain logic, focus on wiring.

## Design

### Data Models

No domain models. This ticket creates infrastructure wiring only.

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `app` | Typer instance | Root CLI application |
| `generate_app` | Typer instance | Subcommand group for `alty generate *` |
| `persona_app` | Typer instance | Subcommand group for `alty persona *` |

### File Structure

```
src/
├── infrastructure/
│   └── cli/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── main.py          # app + subcommand groups + top-level commands
│       ├── generate.py      # generate subcommands (artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs)
│       └── persona.py       # persona subcommands (list, generate)
```

### Sequence / Flow

```
User                  Typer                    Stub Function
  |                     |                          |
  |-- alty init -------->|                          |
  |                     |-- route to init() ------>|
  |                     |                          |-- print "not implemented"
  |                     |<-- exit 0 ---------------|
  |<-- output --------- |
```

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | `main.py` only defines the app and wires subgroups; each command file owns its own stubs |
| **O**pen/Closed | New commands added by defining new functions — no modification to existing stubs |
| **L**iskov Substitution | N/A (no inheritance in stubs) |
| **I**nterface Segregation | Commands split across files by subcommand group (generate.py, persona.py) |
| **D**ependency Inversion | Stubs have no dependencies; when implemented, they will call application ports (Protocols) |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

Write failing tests that verify CLI commands exist and return exit code 0:

```python
# tests/infrastructure/cli/test_cli_entry_point.py
from typer.testing import CliRunner
from src.infrastructure.cli.main import app

runner = CliRunner()

def test_alty_help_shows_all_commands():
    """alty --help lists all 15 commands/groups."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["--help"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert "init" in result.output
    assert "guide" in result.output
    assert "generate" in result.output
    assert "detect" in result.output
    assert "check" in result.output
    assert "kb" in result.output
    assert "doc-health" in result.output
    assert "doc-review" in result.output
    assert "ticket-health" in result.output
    assert "persona" in result.output
    assert "version" in result.output

def test_alty_init_stub_exits_cleanly():
    """alty init prints not-implemented and exits 0."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["init"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0

def test_alty_generate_help_shows_subcommands():
    """alty generate --help lists artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["generate", "--help"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert "artifacts" in result.output
    assert "fitness" in result.output
    assert "tickets" in result.output
    assert "configs" in result.output

def test_alty_persona_help_shows_subcommands():
    """alty persona --help lists list and generate."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["persona", "--help"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert "list" in result.output
    assert "generate" in result.output

def test_alty_version_shows_version():
    """alty version prints the package version."""
    result = runner.invoke(app, ["version"])
    assert result.exit_code == 0
    assert "0.1.0" in result.output
```

Run: `uv run pytest tests/infrastructure/cli/test_cli_entry_point.py -v` — should FAIL

### GREEN Phase

1. Add `typer>=0.24,<1.0` to `pyproject.toml` dependencies
2. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/__init__.py`
3. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/main.py` — define `app`, wire `generate_app` and `persona_app`, add 9 top-level command stubs
4. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/generate.py` — 4 stubs (artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs)
5. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/persona.py` — 2 stubs (list, generate)
6. Add `[project.scripts] alty = "src.infrastructure.cli.main:app"` to pyproject.toml

Run: `uv run pytest tests/infrastructure/cli/test_cli_entry_point.py -v` — should PASS

### REFACTOR Phase

- Verify all command names match ARCHITECTURE.md §6.1 command tree exactly
- Ensure consistent stub output format across all commands
- Check that `--help` text is descriptive per command

## Steps

1. Write CLI entry point tests covering all 15 commands and both subcommand groups.
2. Add `typer>=0.24,<1.0` to pyproject.toml dependencies and run `uv sync`.
3. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/main.py` with root Typer app, generate and persona subgroups, and 9 top-level command stubs (init, guide, detect, check, kb, doc-health, doc-review, ticket-health, version).
4. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/generate.py` with 4 stubs (artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs).
5. Create `src/infrastructure/cli/persona.py` with 2 stubs (list, generate).
6. Wire entry point in pyproject.toml `[project.scripts]`.
7. Run quality gates.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `alty --help` exits 0 and lists all commands (init, guide, generate, detect, check, kb, doc-health, doc-review, ticket-health, persona, version)
- [ ] `alty generate --help` lists subcommands: artifacts, fitness, tickets, configs
- [ ] `alty persona --help` lists subcommands: list, generate
- [ ] Each of the 15 stub commands exits cleanly (exit code 0)
- [ ] `alty version` prints "0.1.0"
- [ ] Entry point `alty = "src.infrastructure.cli.main:app"` is configured in pyproject.toml
- [ ] `typer>=0.24,<1.0` added to project dependencies
- [ ] All tests pass, lint passes, mypy passes

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| No arguments | `alty` (no args) | Show help text (no_args_is_help=True), exit 0 |
| Unknown command | `alty nonexistent` | Typer error message, exit 2 |
| Unknown subcommand | `alty generate nonexistent` | Typer error message, exit 2 |
| Double help | `alty generate artifacts --help` | Show stub help for artifacts, exit 0 |

## Quality Gates

```bash
uv run ruff check src/ tests/
uv run mypy .
uv run pytest tests/ -v --cov=src --cov-fail-under=80
```

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass with >= 80% coverage
- [ ] Edge cases have test coverage

## Risks / Dependencies

- **Risk:** Typer is pre-1.0 (v0.24.1) — API could change. Mitigated by pinning `<1.0` in requirements.
- **Dependency:** Blocks 8 downstream tickets (k7m.15, k7m.16, k7m.17, k7m.22-k7m.26) — this is on the critical path.
- **Dependency:** Blocked by k7m epic (foundation phase must be active).
notes: AC addition (2026-02-23): After completing CLI skeleton, create 17 follow-up tickets from gap analysis design (k7m.8 §13, docs/research/20260223_gap_analysis_design.md). 12 P0 tickets for scaffold overlay, 5 P1 tickets for structural migration. Set formal dependencies per §13 dependency graph.

title: Define application layer port Protocols for all P0 capabilities
description:
## Goal / Problem

Define 13 Protocol classes in `src/application/ports/` that form the contract between the CLI/MCP adapters and the domain layer. Without these Protocols, neither the CLI (k7m.13) nor MCP server (k7m.27) can call into the application layer, and downstream feature tickets (k7m.15, k7m.16, k7m.17) cannot implement their handlers.

## Background / Context

- Spike k7m.4 defined 13 application ports in Section 4 "Shared Core Architecture" (`docs/research/20260222_cli_mcp_design.md`)
- Port → bounded context mapping in spike Section 2 table "Command → Bounded Context → Application Port Mapping"
- DDD.md Section 4 defines bounded contexts with key domain objects referenced by these ports
- DDD.md Section 5 defines aggregate commands that these ports must surface
- **Pattern reference:** Spike Section 4 "Port Example" for DiscoveryPort code snippet
- Ports use `typing.Protocol` (structural subtyping) — no ABC inheritance

### Research Input: k7m.1

Spike k7m.1 refined two port signatures:
- **KnowledgeLookupPort**: concrete signature defined with `lookup()`, `list_tools()`, `list_versions()`, `list_topics()` methods (see Design section below)
- **DocHealthPort**: should include a `--knowledge` mode that reads `[_meta]` sections from TOML entries for drift/staleness detection (per k7m.1 Section 7)

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Cross-cutting (Application Layer) — each port maps to one bounded context |
| Ubiquitous Language | Port, Protocol, Command, Query; plus per-port terms: BootstrapSession, DiscoverySession, DomainModel, FitnessTestSuite, TicketPlan, GlobalSettingConflict, KnowledgeEntry, RippleReview, ToolConfig, Persona |
| Layer | Application (`src/application/ports/`) |

## Design

### Data Models

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `BootstrapPort` | Protocol | Orchestrates `alty init` flow (Bootstrap context) |
| `RescuePort` | Protocol | Orchestrates `alty init --existing` (Rescue context) |
| `DiscoveryPort` | Protocol | Manages guided DDD question session (Guided Discovery context) |
| `ArtifactGenerationPort` | Protocol | Generates PRD, DDD.md, ARCHITECTURE.md (Domain Model context) |
| `FitnessGenerationPort` | Protocol | Generates import-linter + pytestarch tests (Architecture Testing context) |
| `TicketGenerationPort` | Protocol | Generates dependency-ordered beads tickets (Ticket Pipeline context) |
| `ConfigGenerationPort` | Protocol | Generates tool-native configs (Tool Translation context) |
| `ToolDetectionPort` | Protocol | Scans for installed tools and global configs (Bootstrap context) |
| `QualityGatePort` | Protocol | Runs lint + types + tests + fitness (Architecture Testing context) |
| `KnowledgeLookupPort` | Protocol | RLM knowledge base queries (Knowledge Base context) |
| `DocHealthPort` | Protocol | Doc freshness + health report (Knowledge Base context) |
| `TicketHealthPort` | Protocol | Ripple review report (Ticket Freshness context) |
| `PersonaPort` | Protocol | List and generate agent persona configs (Tool Translation context) |

### File Structure

```
src/application/
├── __init__.py
└── ports/
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── bootstrap_port.py
    ├── rescue_port.py
    ├── discovery_port.py
    ├── artifact_generation_port.py
    ├── fitness_generation_port.py
    ├── ticket_generation_port.py
    ├── config_generation_port.py
    ├── tool_detection_port.py
    ├── quality_gate_port.py
    ├── knowledge_lookup_port.py
    ├── doc_health_port.py
    ├── ticket_health_port.py
    └── persona_port.py
```

### Sequence / Flow

```
CLI / MCP Adapter         Application Port (Protocol)       Domain Model
      |                            |                            |
      |-- call port method ------->|                            |
      |                            |-- (future: call domain) -->|
      |                            |                            |
      |<-- return result ---------|                            |
```

At this stage, ports define the interface only. Concrete implementations come in downstream tickets.

### Port Method Signatures (Key Examples)

```python
# BootstrapPort
class BootstrapPort(Protocol):
    def preview(self, project_dir: Path) -> Preview: ...
    def confirm(self, session_id: str) -> Confirmation: ...
    def execute(self, session_id: str) -> BootstrapCompleted: ...

# DiscoveryPort (from spike example)
class DiscoveryPort(Protocol):
    def start_session(self, readme_content: str) -> str: ...
    def detect_persona(self, session_id: str, choice: str) -> str: ...
    def answer_question(self, session_id: str, answer: str) -> str: ...
    def confirm_playback(self, session_id: str, confirmed: bool) -> str: ...
    def complete(self, session_id: str) -> str: ...

# KnowledgeLookupPort (refined by k7m.1 spike)
class KnowledgeLookupPort(Protocol):
    def lookup(self, category: str, topic: str, version: str = "current") -> KnowledgeEntry: ...
    def list_tools(self) -> list[ToolMeta]: ...
    def list_versions(self, tool: str) -> list[str]: ...
    def list_topics(self, category: str, tool: str | None = None) -> list[str]: ...

# ToolDetectionPort
class ToolDetectionPort(Protocol):
    def detect(self, project_dir: Path) -> list[str]: ...
    def scan_conflicts(self, project_dir: Path) -> list[str]: ...
```

Note: Return types use `str` placeholders where domain value objects are not yet defined. Downstream tickets will refine types as domain models are implemented. Exception: KnowledgeLookupPort signature is concrete per k7m.1 spike findings.

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | Each Protocol file defines exactly one port for one bounded context |
| **O**pen/Closed | New ports added as new files — existing ports untouched |
| **L**iskov Substitution | Any class implementing a Protocol must honor its contract (structural typing enforces this) |
| **I**nterface Segregation | 13 focused Protocols instead of one monolithic interface — each has only the methods its context needs |
| **D**ependency Inversion | CLI and MCP depend on Protocols, never on concrete implementations |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

Write tests that verify Protocol classes exist, are importable, and define expected methods:

```python
# tests/application/ports/test_ports_exist.py
import inspect
from typing import Protocol

def test_bootstrap_port_is_protocol():
    from src.application.ports.bootstrap_port import BootstrapPort
    assert issubclass(BootstrapPort, Protocol)

def test_bootstrap_port_has_preview_method():
    from src.application.ports.bootstrap_port import BootstrapPort
    assert hasattr(BootstrapPort, "preview")

def test_discovery_port_has_required_methods():
    from src.application.ports.discovery_port import DiscoveryPort
    methods = ["start_session", "detect_persona", "answer_question", "confirm_playback", "complete"]
    for method in methods:
        assert hasattr(DiscoveryPort, method), f"DiscoveryPort missing {method}"

def test_knowledge_lookup_port_has_k7m1_methods():
    from src.application.ports.knowledge_lookup_port import KnowledgeLookupPort
    methods = ["lookup", "list_tools", "list_versions", "list_topics"]
    for method in methods:
        assert hasattr(KnowledgeLookupPort, method), f"KnowledgeLookupPort missing {method}"

def test_tool_detection_port_has_detect_and_scan():
    from src.application.ports.tool_detection_port import ToolDetectionPort
    assert hasattr(ToolDetectionPort, "detect")
    assert hasattr(ToolDetectionPort, "scan_conflicts")

def test_all_13_ports_importable():
    """Every port file must be importable."""
    from src.application.ports import bootstrap_port
    from src.application.ports import rescue_port
    from src.application.ports import discovery_port
    from src.application.ports import artifact_generation_port
    from src.application.ports import fitness_generation_port
    from src.application.ports import ticket_generation_port
    from src.application.ports import config_generation_port
    from src.application.ports import tool_detection_port
    from src.application.ports import quality_gate_port
    from src.application.ports import knowledge_lookup_port
    from src.application.ports import doc_health_port
    from src.application.ports import ticket_health_port
    from src.application.ports import persona_port

def test_ports_have_no_external_dependencies():
    """Port modules must not import anything outside stdlib and domain."""
    # Verify no typer, no mcp, no requests, no DB imports
    from src.application.ports import bootstrap_port
    source = inspect.getsource(bootstrap_port)
    forbidden = ["typer", "mcp", "requests", "sqlalchemy", "fastapi"]
    for lib in forbidden:
        assert lib not in source, f"Port imports forbidden library: {lib}"
```

Run: `uv run pytest tests/application/ports/test_ports_exist.py -v` — should FAIL

### GREEN Phase

1. Create `src/application/__init__.py`
2. Create `src/application/ports/__init__.py`
3. Create all 13 port files, each defining one Protocol class with method signatures
4. Methods use `str` or simple types as return type placeholders (refined in downstream tickets)
5. KnowledgeLookupPort uses concrete signature from k7m.1: `lookup()`, `list_tools()`, `list_versions()`, `list_topics()`

Run: `uv run pytest tests/application/ports/test_ports_exist.py -v` — should PASS

### REFACTOR Phase

- Verify method names match DDD.md aggregate commands (e.g., DiscoveryPort methods map to DiscoverySession commands)
- Ensure docstrings reference the bounded context and aggregate
- Group related port re-exports in `__init__.py` for convenience imports

## Steps

1. Write tests verifying all 13 Protocols are importable, are Protocols, and define expected methods.
2. Create `src/application/__init__.py` and `src/application/ports/__init__.py`.
3. Create 13 Protocol files with method signatures derived from DDD.md aggregate commands and spike port mapping.
4. Ensure zero external dependencies in port files (stdlib + domain types only).
5. Run quality gates.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 13 Protocol classes exist in `src/application/ports/` and are importable
- [ ] Each Protocol uses `typing.Protocol` (not ABC)
- [ ] Method signatures align with DDD.md aggregate commands for their bounded context
- [ ] KnowledgeLookupPort uses concrete signature from k7m.1: `lookup(category, topic, version)`, `list_tools()`, `list_versions(tool)`, `list_topics(category, tool)`
- [ ] DocHealthPort includes a `--knowledge` mode that reads `[_meta]` sections from TOML entries for drift detection (per k7m.1 Section 7)
- [ ] Port files have zero external dependencies (no typer, mcp, requests, sqlalchemy, etc.)
- [ ] Each Protocol has a module-level docstring referencing its bounded context
- [ ] All 13 ports are listed in `src/application/ports/__init__.py` for convenience
- [ ] mypy strict mode passes on all port files
- [ ] All tests pass, lint passes

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| Protocol with wrong signature | Class missing a required method | mypy error when used as implementation |
| Import from wrong layer | Port tries to import from infrastructure | Test fails (forbidden import check) |
| Duplicate method names | Two ports define same method name | Allowed — Protocols are separate interfaces |
| Empty Protocol | Protocol with no methods | Not allowed — every port must have at least one method |

## Quality Gates

```bash
uv run ruff check src/ tests/
uv run mypy .
uv run pytest tests/ -v --cov=src --cov-fail-under=80
```

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes (mypy strict)
- [ ] All tests pass with >= 80% coverage
- [ ] Edge cases have test coverage

## Risks / Dependencies

- **Risk:** Return types are placeholder strings until domain value objects exist. Downstream tickets (k7m.15, k7m.17) will refine signatures.
- **Risk:** Protocol method signatures may need adjustment as domain models solidify. Keep methods minimal — easier to add than remove.
- **Dependency:** Blocks k7m.15 (alty init), k7m.16 (alty detect), k7m.17 (alty guide), k7m.27 (MCP server).
- **Dependency:** Blocked by k7m epic (foundation phase must be active).
notes: AC addition (2026-02-23): After completing port definitions, create 17 follow-up tickets from gap analysis design (k7m.8 §13, docs/research/20260223_gap_analysis_design.md). Several rescue-related ports (RescuePort, GapAnalysis) are defined in §10 of the report and need to be reflected in the port Protocols.

title: Implement alty init (new project bootstrap flow)
description:
## Goal / Problem

Implement the `alty init` command — the primary user-facing entry point that orchestrates new project bootstrap. A user with a README idea runs `alty init` and gets: tool detection, preview of changes, confirmation, guided discovery, artifact generation, fitness function generation, ticket generation, and config generation. Without this, alty has no end-to-end bootstrap flow.

## Background / Context

- PRD Section 5.2 defines `alty init` behavior: preview first, confirm, never overwrite, conflict resolution
- DDD.md Section 4 "Bootstrap (Orchestration)" context defines BootstrapSession aggregate
- DDD.md Section 5 "Aggregate: BootstrapSession" — commands: preview, confirm, execute; events: BootstrapCompleted
- Domain Story 1 (DDD.md Section 1) defines the 21-step bootstrap flow
- PRD Section 5.2.1 defines global settings detection behavior
- Spike k7m.4 Section 2 maps `alty init` to BootstrapPort
- **Pattern reference:** DDD.md aggregate invariant "Preview before action is a hard constraint"
- **Hard constraint from PRD:** Never overwrite existing files; conflict rename with `_alty` suffix

### Research Input: k7m.1

Spike k7m.1 findings affect the bootstrap flow in two ways:
- **Knowledge directory scaffolding**: `alty init` must scaffold `.alty/knowledge/` during bootstrap — copy TOML files from knowledge base, create `_meta.toml` and `_index.toml`, create `current/` dirs for each detected tool
- **AGENTS.md generation**: AGENTS.md should be generated as a cross-tool bridge file alongside tool-specific configs, since k7m.1 found that Cursor, Roo Code, and OpenCode all support AGENTS.md as a shared convention file

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Bootstrap (Orchestration) |
| Ubiquitous Language | BootstrapSession, Preview, Confirmation, GlobalSettingConflict, BootstrapCompleted, Seed |
| Layer | Domain (BootstrapSession aggregate) + Application (BootstrapHandler) + Infrastructure (CLI adapter in `main.py`) |

## Design

### Data Models

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `BootstrapSession` | Aggregate (Root) | Tracks state of a `alty init` run; enforces preview-before-action invariant |
| `Preview` | Value Object | Immutable snapshot of what will be created/installed/skipped |
| `Confirmation` | Value Object | User's explicit approval to proceed |
| `GlobalSettingConflict` | Value Object | A detected conflict between global tool config and local settings (tool, global_path, global_value, local_value, resolution) |
| `BootstrapCompleted` | Domain Event | Emitted when project is fully seeded |
| `FileAction` | Value Object | One planned file operation: CREATE, SKIP (exists), or CONFLICT_RENAME |

### Sequence / Flow

```
User            CLI (Typer)         BootstrapHandler       BootstrapSession       ToolDetectionPort
  |                 |                     |                      |                      |
  |-- alty init ----->|                     |                      |                      |
  |                 |-- execute() ------->|                      |                      |
  |                 |                     |-- detect() -------------------------------->|
  |                 |                     |<-- tools + conflicts -------------------------|
  |                 |                     |-- create_session() ->|                      |
  |                 |                     |                      |-- set_preview()      |
  |                 |<-- show preview ----|<-- Preview ----------|                      |
  |<-- preview -----|                     |                      |                      |
  |-- confirm ----->|                     |                      |                      |
  |                 |-- confirm() ------->|-- confirm() -------->|                      |
  |                 |                     |                      |-- validate invariant |
  |                 |                     |-- orchestrate ------->|                      |
  |                 |                     |   (guide, generate,  |                      |
  |                 |                     |    fitness, tickets,  |                      |
  |                 |                     |    configs)           |                      |
  |                 |                     |<-- BootstrapCompleted |                      |
  |<-- summary -----|<-- result ---------|                      |                      |
```

### BootstrapSession State Machine

```
CREATED -> PREVIEWED -> CONFIRMED -> EXECUTING -> COMPLETED
                  \-> CANCELLED (user says no)
```

### Bootstrap Steps (updated with k7m.1 findings)

The execute phase includes the following steps in order:
1. Guided discovery (via DiscoveryPort)
2. Artifact generation (PRD, DDD.md, ARCHITECTURE.md)
3. Fitness function generation
4. Ticket generation
5. **Knowledge directory scaffolding** — create `.alty/knowledge/` structure: copy TOML files from knowledge base, create `_meta.toml` and `_index.toml`, create `current/` dirs for detected tools
6. Tool-specific config generation (`.claude/`, `.cursor/`, etc.)
7. **AGENTS.md generation** — generate as cross-tool bridge file (Cursor, Roo Code, OpenCode all support AGENTS.md per k7m.1)

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | BootstrapSession manages session state and invariants only; orchestration logic in BootstrapHandler; CLI display in Typer adapter |
| **O**pen/Closed | New bootstrap steps (e.g., future MCP config) added via new port calls in handler — BootstrapSession unchanged |
| **L**iskov Substitution | BootstrapHandler depends on port Protocols; any implementation (real or test double) works |
| **I**nterface Segregation | Bootstrap uses ToolDetectionPort for detection, separate from DiscoveryPort for guided questions |
| **D**ependency Inversion | BootstrapHandler depends on BootstrapPort Protocol; CLI depends on handler, never on domain directly |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

```python
# tests/domain/models/test_bootstrap_session.py
def test_bootstrap_session_starts_in_created_state():
    """New BootstrapSession has status CREATED."""

def test_preview_transitions_to_previewed():
    """set_preview() moves session from CREATED to PREVIEWED."""

def test_confirm_requires_preview_first():
    """confirm() on CREATED session raises InvariantViolation."""

def test_confirm_transitions_to_confirmed():
    """confirm() on PREVIEWED session moves to CONFIRMED."""

def test_cancel_transitions_to_cancelled():
    """cancel() on PREVIEWED session moves to CANCELLED."""

def test_execute_requires_confirmation():
    """begin_execution() on PREVIEWED (not confirmed) raises InvariantViolation."""

def test_complete_produces_bootstrap_completed_event():
    """complete() emits BootstrapCompleted domain event."""

def test_preview_contains_file_actions():
    """Preview lists CREATE, SKIP, and CONFLICT_RENAME actions."""

def test_preview_includes_knowledge_directory_scaffolding():
    """Preview includes CREATE actions for .alty/knowledge/ structure."""

def test_preview_includes_agents_md_generation():
    """Preview includes CREATE action for AGENTS.md cross-tool bridge file."""

def test_global_setting_conflict_detected():
    """Preview includes GlobalSettingConflict when global config overrides local."""

# tests/domain/models/test_preview.py
def test_preview_never_overwrites_existing_files():
    """FileAction for existing file is SKIP, not CREATE."""

def test_preview_conflict_rename_uses_alty_suffix():
    """Conflicting file renamed to filename_alty.ext."""

# tests/application/commands/test_bootstrap_handler.py
def test_bootstrap_handler_calls_tool_detection():
    """Handler calls ToolDetectionPort.detect() during preview phase."""

def test_bootstrap_handler_orchestrates_full_flow():
    """Handler calls detect -> preview -> confirm -> (guide, generate, fitness, tickets, knowledge scaffolding, configs, AGENTS.md)."""
```

Run: `uv run pytest tests/domain/models/test_bootstrap_session.py -v` — should FAIL

### GREEN Phase

1. Create `src/domain/models/bootstrap_session.py` — BootstrapSession aggregate with state machine
2. Create `src/domain/models/preview.py` — Preview, FileAction, GlobalSettingConflict value objects
3. Create `src/domain/events/bootstrap_events.py` — BootstrapCompleted event
4. Create `src/application/commands/bootstrap_handler.py` — BootstrapHandler orchestrating ports
5. Wire `alty init` command in `src/infrastructure/cli/main.py` to call BootstrapHandler

Run: `uv run pytest tests/domain/models/test_bootstrap_session.py -v` — should PASS

### REFACTOR Phase

- Verify BootstrapSession state transitions match DDD.md aggregate design
- Ensure Preview value object is immutable (frozen dataclass or similar)
- Verify GlobalSettingConflict matches PRD Section 5.2.1 behavior exactly
- Check ubiquitous language: class names must match DDD.md glossary terms

## Steps

1. Write domain tests for BootstrapSession aggregate (state machine, invariants, events).
2. Write domain tests for Preview, FileAction, GlobalSettingConflict value objects.
3. Implement BootstrapSession in `src/domain/models/bootstrap_session.py`.
4. Implement Preview and related value objects in `src/domain/models/preview.py`.
5. Implement BootstrapCompleted event in `src/domain/events/bootstrap_events.py`.
6. Write application tests for BootstrapHandler orchestration.
7. Implement BootstrapHandler in `src/application/commands/bootstrap_handler.py`.
8. Wire `alty init` CLI command to BootstrapHandler (replace stub from k7m.13).
9. **Include `.alty/knowledge/` scaffolding step** — copy TOML files, create `_meta.toml`, `_index.toml`, `current/` dirs for detected tools.
10. **Include AGENTS.md generation** as cross-tool bridge file alongside tool-specific configs.
11. Write CLI integration test for `alty init` end-to-end (with test doubles for ports).
12. Run quality gates.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] BootstrapSession aggregate enforces preview-before-action invariant (cannot confirm without preview, cannot execute without confirmation)
- [ ] Preview lists all planned file actions (CREATE, SKIP, CONFLICT_RENAME) as immutable value objects
- [ ] GlobalSettingConflict detected when global tool config would override local settings
- [ ] Never overwrites existing files — existing files get SKIP action in Preview
- [ ] Conflicting files renamed with `_alty` suffix
- [ ] `alty init` shows preview, waits for confirmation, then orchestrates the full flow
- [ ] BootstrapCompleted domain event emitted on successful completion
- [ ] **`.alty/knowledge/` directory scaffolded during bootstrap** — TOML files copied from knowledge base, `_meta.toml` and `_index.toml` created, `current/` dirs for detected tools
- [ ] **AGENTS.md generated as cross-tool bridge file** alongside tool-specific configs (Cursor, Roo Code, OpenCode all support AGENTS.md per k7m.1)
- [ ] Domain layer (BootstrapSession, Preview, GlobalSettingConflict) has zero external dependencies
- [ ] All tests pass, lint passes, mypy passes

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| Empty project dir | `alty init` in empty directory | Preview shows all CREATE actions, no SKIP/CONFLICT |
| All files exist | `alty init` where everything already exists | Preview shows all SKIP, nothing to create |
| User cancels | User says "no" at confirmation | Session moves to CANCELLED, no files written |
| Global conflict with compatible value | Global and local settings agree | Conflict marked as "OK, compatible" |
| Global conflict with incompatible value | Global overrides local | Conflict shown with resolution options |
| No README found | `alty init` without README.md | Error: "Create a README.md with your project idea first" |
| Dirty git working tree | Uncommitted changes present | Warning shown but proceed (--existing is the one that blocks) |
| Knowledge dir already exists | `.alty/knowledge/` already present | SKIP existing files, CREATE only missing ones |
| AGENTS.md already exists | AGENTS.md already in project root | SKIP (never overwrite), or CONFLICT_RENAME if content differs |

## Quality Gates

```bash
uv run ruff check src/ tests/
uv run mypy .
uv run pytest tests/ -v --cov=src --cov-fail-under=80
```

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass with >= 80% coverage
- [ ] Edge cases have test coverage
- [ ] Domain layer has no external dependencies

## Risks / Dependencies

- **Dependency:** Requires k7m.13 (CLI entry point with stubs) and k7m.14 (port Protocols) to be complete.
- **Dependency:** Blocks k7m.28 (alty init --existing / rescue flow).
- **Risk:** Full orchestration depends on ports that will have stub implementations initially. Use test doubles (fakes) for unimplemented ports during testing.
- **Risk:** The guided discovery flow (k7m.17) is a separate ticket. `alty init` must call DiscoveryPort but the real implementation comes later. Wire with a pass-through stub for now.
- **Risk:** Knowledge base TOML content must exist before bootstrap can scaffold `.alty/knowledge/`. If knowledge base is not yet populated, scaffolding creates empty directory structure with placeholder `_meta.toml` files.
- **Scope:** This ticket implements the Bootstrap aggregate and handler. It does NOT implement guided discovery, artifact generation, fitness generation, ticket generation, or config generation — those are separate tickets that plug into the ports.
